How Machine Learning is Transforming Web Search

By Jane Smith | January 15, 2024

The landscape of web search has changed dramatically over the past decade. Traditional keyword-based search engines relied on exact word matching and simple ranking algorithms like TF-IDF and PageRank. Today, companies like Google, Bing, and OpenAI are deploying large language models and vector embedding systems that understand the semantic meaning behind a query.

The Rise of Semantic Search

Semantic search goes beyond keyword matching. When you search for "best programming language for data science", a semantic search engine understands that you probably want information about Python, R, Julia, and similar tools — even if your exact query words don't appear in the document.

This is achieved through dense vector embeddings — mathematical representations of text in high-dimensional space where semantically similar texts are geometrically close to each other. Models like BERT, RoBERTa, and the sentence-transformers library produce these embeddings efficiently.

How It Works in Practice

Modern search systems use a two-stage retrieval approach: first, a fast approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search retrieves candidate documents from the vector index; then a more expensive cross-encoder reranks the top candidates for precision.

Companies like Pinecone, Weaviate, and Qdrant have built specialised vector databases that can store and search billions of embeddings in milliseconds. These systems use algorithms like HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graphs that achieve sub-millisecond query latency at massive scale.

Real-Time Indexing Challenges

One of the hardest problems in modern search is real-time indexing. The web publishes millions of new pages every hour. Getting a new page into the search index quickly — within 60 seconds of publication — requires a sophisticated pipeline involving URL discovery via WebSub and certificate transparency logs, distributed crawling, and streaming NLP processing.
